title: An extension of the theory of planned behavior to predict pedestrians' violating crossing behavior using structural equation modeling
authors: Hongmei Zhou; Stephanie Ballon Romero; Xiao Qin
research_problem: This paper examines pedestrians' self-reported violating crossing behavior intentions by applying the Theory of Planned Behavior (TPB). The study extends TPB by adding new factors including descriptive norm, perceived risk and conformity tendency to evaluate their respective impacts on pedestrians' behavior intentions. The survey used a scenario where pedestrians crossed the road violating pedestrian lights at an intersection in Dalian, China.
method_summary: The study used a questionnaire with scenario-based items about pedestrians crossing the road violating pedestrian lights at an intersection. Survey was conducted in Dalian, China with 260 complete and valid responses. Data was analyzed using Structural Equation Modeling (SEM) with maximum likelihood estimation. Seven constructs were measured: behavioral intention, instrumental attitude, subjective norm, perceived behavioral control, descriptive norm, perceived risk, and conformity tendency.
tasks: Predicting pedestrian violating crossing behavior intentions
datasets: Survey data from 260 respondents in Dalian, China
metrics: CMIN/df ratio; RMSEA (Root Mean Square Error of Approximation); PCLOS E (p-value for RMSEA); CFI (Comparative Fit Index); Regression weights; Cronbach's alpha for reliability
claim_text: Instrumental attitude was significant in all four models, indicating pedestrians are willing to take risks to cross the road if this behavior is associated with meaningful benefits like saving time or providing convenience
limitations: Relatively small number of items in each construct to maintain internal reliability of the data could affect the results | Bias of reported behavior, especially negative behavior, as actual behavior was not observed for comparison or validation | Perceived behavioral control was not significant - this may be because the scenario did not represent a sufficiently challenging situation; results might differ with more risky scenarios | Perceived risk was not a significant predictor - pedestrians may not adequately perceive the susceptibility and seriousness of the risk pertaining to such violations
notes: The study was supported by the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities of China (Grant No. DUT14RW132) and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 51308092). The survey was conducted in Dalian, China with 260 respondents; 57.3% female, 49.2% aged 18-24 years, 58.5% with undergraduate degree.

title: An extension of the theory of planned behavior to predict pedestrians' violating crossing behavior using structural equation modeling
authors: Hongmei Zhou; Stephanie Ballon Romero; Xiao Qin
research_problem: This paper examines pedestrians' self-reported violating crossing behavior intentions by applying the Theory of Planned Behavior (TPB). The study extends TPB by adding new factors including descriptive norm, perceived risk and conformity tendency to evaluate their respective impacts on pedestrians' behavior intentions. The survey used a scenario where pedestrians crossed the road violating pedestrian lights at an intersection in Dalian, China.
method_summary: The study used a questionnaire with scenario-based items about pedestrians crossing the road violating pedestrian lights at an intersection. Survey was conducted in Dalian, China with 260 complete and valid responses. Data was analyzed using Structural Equation Modeling (SEM) with maximum likelihood estimation. Seven constructs were measured: behavioral intention, instrumental attitude, subjective norm, perceived behavioral control, descriptive norm, perceived risk, and conformity tendency.
tasks: Predicting pedestrian violating crossing behavior intentions
datasets: Survey data from 260 respondents in Dalian, China
metrics: CMIN/df ratio; RMSEA (Root Mean Square Error of Approximation); PCLOS E (p-value for RMSEA); CFI (Comparative Fit Index); Regression weights; Cronbach's alpha for reliability
claim_text: Model 4 (containing instrumental attitude, descriptive norm, and conformity tendency) provided the best predictive utility with the best goodness of fit (CFI=0.996, CMIN/DF=1.235, RMSEA=0.030)
limitations: Relatively small number of items in each construct to maintain internal reliability of the data could affect the results | Bias of reported behavior, especially negative behavior, as actual behavior was not observed for comparison or validation | Perceived behavioral control was not significant - this may be because the scenario did not represent a sufficiently challenging situation; results might differ with more risky scenarios | Perceived risk was not a significant predictor - pedestrians may not adequately perceive the susceptibility and seriousness of the risk pertaining to such violations
notes: The study was supported by the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities of China (Grant No. DUT14RW132) and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 51308092). The survey was conducted in Dalian, China with 260 respondents; 57.3% female, 49.2% aged 18-24 years, 58.5% with undergraduate degree.

title: An extension of the theory of planned behavior to predict pedestrians' violating crossing behavior using structural equation modeling
authors: Hongmei Zhou; Stephanie Ballon Romero; Xiao Qin
research_problem: This paper examines pedestrians' self-reported violating crossing behavior intentions by applying the Theory of Planned Behavior (TPB). The study extends TPB by adding new factors including descriptive norm, perceived risk and conformity tendency to evaluate their respective impacts on pedestrians' behavior intentions. authors: Hongmei Zhou; Stephanie Ballon Romero; Xiao Qin
research_problem: This paper examines pedestrians' self-reported violating crossing behavior intentions by applying the Theory of Planned Behavior (TPB). title: An extension of the theory of planned behavior to predict pedestrians' violating crossing behavior using structural equation modeling
authors: Hongmei Zhou; Stephanie Ballon Romero; Xiao Qin
research_problem: This paper examines pedestrians' self-reported violating crossing behavior intentions by applying the Theory of Planned Behavior (TPB). The study extends TPB by adding new factors including descriptive norm, perceived risk and conformity tendency to evaluate their respective impacts on pedestrians' behavior intentions. metrics: CMIN/df ratio; RMSEA (Root Mean Square Error of Approximation); PCLOS E (p-value for RMSEA); CFI (Comparative Fit Index); Regression weights; Cronbach's alpha for reliability

notes: The study was supported by the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities of China (Grant No. DUT14RW132) and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 51308092). The survey was conducted in Dalian, China with 260 respondents; 57.3% female, 49.2% aged 18-24 years, 58.5% with undergraduate degree.

title: Effects of mobile phone distraction on pedestrians' crossing behavior and visual attention allocation at a signalized intersection: An outdoor experimental study
authors: Kang Jiang; Feiyang Ling; Zhongxiang Feng; Changxi Ma; Wesley Kumfer; Chen Shao; Kun Wang
research_problem: Investigates how mobile phone distractions (music, phone conversation, and text) affect pedestrian crossing behavior and visual attention allocation at a signalized intersection
method_summary: Outdoor-environment experiment with 28 college student pedestrians using two HD videos and Tobii Pro Glasses 2 eye tracker to record crossing behavior and visual attention under four conditions: undistracted, music distraction, phone conversation distraction, and text distraction
tasks: Pedestrian crossing behavior under mobile phone distraction
datasets: 28 college students at signalized intersection in Hefei, China
metrics: Initiate time; Look left and right frequency; Crossing speed; Pupil diameter; Scanning frequency; Fixation points; Fixation time; Mean fixation duration
claim_text: Text distraction causes the greatest impairment to pedestrian crossing performance, followed by phone conversation distraction and music distraction
limitations: Small sample size (n=28) limits generalizability | Sample limited to college students (ages 17-25), may not represent overall pedestrian population | Participants restricted from self-regulation and behavioral adaptation in experiment, may not reflect daily life behavior | Data collected at one specific signalized intersection with very low traffic volume, may not generalize to complex intersections | Use of unfamiliar phone and mental arithmetic tasks may not conform to participants' daily life mobile phone use patterns | Only three types of mobile phone distractions studied (music, conversation, text), other tasks like mobile internet not examined
notes: Outdoor experimental study using Tobii Pro Glasses 2 for eye tracking; found text distraction most harmful, followed by phone conversation and music; research funded by National Natural Science Foundation of China

title: Exploring factors affecting pedestrians' red-light running behaviors at intersections in China
authors: Weihua Zhang; Kun Wang; Lei Wang; Zhongxiang Feng; Yingjie Du
research_problem: Exploring how various factors affect pedestrians' red-light running behaviors at intersection areas using data collected from Hefei, China
method_summary: A questionnaire was designed to collect pedestrians' socioeconomic characteristics, trip-related features, and attitude variables. Based on 631 valid samples, a binomial logistic regression model was established using the Score test for variable selection to evaluate the impacts of contributing factors on pedestrians' red-light running behavior.
tasks: Identify factors affecting pedestrian red-light running behavior at signalized intersections
datasets: 631 valid survey samples collected from six signalized intersections in Hefei, China during May-June 2015
metrics: Score test, odds ratio, p-values, 95% confidence intervals
claim_text: Four variables significantly affect pedestrian red-light running probability: trip purpose, time period in a day, pedestrian's attitude towards running red light when in hurry, and pedestrian's attitude towards quality of road facility affecting crossing behavior
limitations: Data were collected in only one Chinese city (Hefei); results may not be exactly the same in other cities or countries due to differences in culture and driving behaviors | Study only considered pedestrians' red-light running behaviors at signalized intersection areas, excluding other crossing facility types | Study focused on individual crossing behaviors while ignoring the impacts of pedestrian platoons | Sample size limited to 631 respondents from six intersections; more data from multiple intersections in other cities would strengthen findings
notes: The study addresses a critical traffic safety issue in China where pedestrian-involved traffic crashes account for a quarter of all traffic deaths. The binomial logistic model uses Score test for variable selection at 95% confidence level. Key finding: pedestrians' attitudes (not just socioeconomic factors) significantly influence red-light running behavior.

title: Exploring factors affecting pedestrians' red-light running behaviors at intersections in China

notes: The study addresses a critical traffic safety issue in China where pedestrian-involved traffic crashes account for a quarter of all traffic deaths. The binomial logistic model uses Score test for variable selection at 95% confidence level. Key finding: pedestrians' attitudes (not just socioeconomic factors) significantly influence red-light running behavior.

title: Modeling pedestrian crossing speed profiles considering speed change behavior for the safety assessment of signalized intersections
research_problem: Pedestrian safety at signalized intersections is threatened by unexpected sudden speed changes (acceleration/deceleration) that pedestrians exhibit while crossing. These behavioral changes cannot be anticipated by conflicting vehicles, potentially leading to hazardous situations and pedestrian-vehicle conflicts. The study aims to quantitatively model these sudden speed changes to improve safety assessment at signalized crosswalks.
method_summary: The study develops a pedestrian speed profile model using stepwise functions. A discrete choice model (multinomial logit) determines the probability of acceleration, deceleration, or constant speed at each time interval based on factors including necessary speed to complete crossing (Vnec - Vcur), conflict area presence, and signal timing. Separate regression models are used for acceleration (linear regression) and deceleration (normal distribution). Monte Carlo simulation generates complete speed profiles. Data from video surveys at 5 crosswalks in Nagoya, Japan was used.
method_family: Discrete choice model with regression analysis
tasks: Pedestrian speed profile modeling, crossing time estimation, vehicle-pedestrian conflict simulation
datasets: Video survey data from 5 crosswalks at 3 intersections in Nagoya City, Japan (Kanayama, Ueda, Fushimi)
metrics: Modified rho-squared (0.431), Modified R² (acceleration: 0.765, deceleration: 0.725), vehicle yield rate, Post Encroachment Time (PET)
claim_text: The proposed model represents pedestrian travel time distribution more accurately than the constant speed model
limitations: The accuracy of individual speed-change event detection needs further improvement (23.6% error for non-acceleration, 43.8% for acceleration events) | Pedestrian-pedestrian interaction is not considered because pedestrian traffic volumes at study sites were relatively low | Pedestrian characteristics such as age, gender, and distraction (e.g., smartphone use) were not considered in the model | Limited sample size from only five sites prevented some expected variables (PFG/PG length, crosswalk length, elapsed time from PFG onset) from being significant | The model does not consider real interaction between pedestrians and turning vehicles
notes: The study was supported by the Memorandum of Understanding between Qatar Transportation and Traffic Safety Center at Qatar University and the International Center of Urban Safety Engineering at the University of Tokyo. Data was collected using the TrafficAnalyzer video image processing system.

title: Modeling pedestrian crossing speed profiles considering speed change behavior for the safety assessment of signalized intersections
research_problem: Pedestrian safety at signalized intersections is threatened by unexpected sudden speed changes (acceleration/deceleration) that pedestrians exhibit while crossing. These behavioral changes cannot be anticipated by conflicting vehicles, potentially leading to hazardous situations and pedestrian-vehicle conflicts. The study aims to quantitatively model these sudden speed changes to improve safety assessment at signalized crosswalks.
method_summary: The study develops a pedestrian speed profile model using stepwise functions. A discrete choice model (multinomial logit) determines the probability of acceleration, deceleration, or constant speed at each time interval based on factors including necessary speed to complete crossing (Vnec - Vcur), conflict area presence, and signal timing. Separate regression models are used for acceleration (linear regression) and deceleration (normal distribution). Monte Carlo simulation generates complete speed profiles. Data from video surveys at 5 crosswalks in Nagoya, Japan was used.
method_family: Discrete choice model with regression analysis
tasks: Pedestrian speed profile modeling, crossing time estimation, vehicle-pedestrian conflict simulation
datasets: Video survey data from 5 crosswalks at 3 intersections in Nagoya City, Japan (Kanayama, Ueda, Fushimi)
metrics: Modified rho-squared (0.431), Modified R² (acceleration: 0.765, deceleration: 0.725), vehicle yield rate, Post Encroachment Time (PET)
claim_text: The difference between necessary speed to complete crossing and current speed (Vnec - Vcur), conflict area dummy, and pedestrian flashing green (PFG) phase have significant impacts on acceleration/deceleration choices
limitations: The accuracy of individual speed-change event detection needs further improvement (23.6% error for non-acceleration, 43.8% for acceleration events) | Pedestrian-pedestrian interaction is not considered because pedestrian traffic volumes at study sites were relatively low | Pedestrian characteristics such as age, gender, and distraction (e.g., smartphone use) were not considered in the model | Limited sample size from only five sites prevented some expected variables (PFG/PG length, crosswalk length, elapsed time from PFG onset) from being significant | The model does not consider real interaction between pedestrians and turning vehicles
notes: The study was supported by the Memorandum of Understanding between Qatar Transportation and Traffic Safety Center at Qatar University and the International Center of Urban Safety Engineering at the University of Tokyo. Data was collected using the TrafficAnalyzer video image processing system.

title: Modeling pedestrian crossing speed profiles considering speed change behavior for the safety assessment of signalized intersections
research_problem: Pedestrian safety at signalized intersections is threatened by unexpected sudden speed changes (acceleration/deceleration) that pedestrians exhibit while crossing. These behavioral changes cannot be anticipated by conflicting vehicles, potentially leading to hazardous situations and pedestrian-vehicle conflicts. The study aims to quantitatively model these sudden speed changes to improve safety assessment at signalized crosswalks.
method_summary: The study develops a pedestrian speed profile model using stepwise functions. A discrete choice model (multinomial logit) determines the probability of acceleration, deceleration, or constant speed at each time interval based on factors including necessary speed to complete crossing (Vnec - Vcur), conflict area presence, and signal timing. Separate regression models are used for acceleration (linear regression) and deceleration (normal distribution). Monte Carlo simulation generates complete speed profiles. Data from video surveys at 5 crosswalks in Nagoya, Japan was used.
method_family: Discrete choice model with regression analysis
tasks: Pedestrian speed profile modeling, crossing time estimation, vehicle-pedestrian conflict simulation
datasets: Video survey data from 5 crosswalks at 3 intersections in Nagoya City, Japan (Kanayama, Ueda, Fushimi)
metrics: Modified rho-squared (0.431), Modified R² (acceleration: 0.765, deceleration: 0.725), vehicle yield rate, Post Encroachment Time (PET)
claim_text: The pedestrian speed change model provides significantly higher vehicle yield rates compared to constant speed assumptions, demonstrating its importance for safety assessment
limitations: The accuracy of individual speed-change event detection needs further improvement (23.6% error for non-acceleration, 43.8% for acceleration events) | Pedestrian-pedestrian interaction is not considered because pedestrian traffic volumes at study sites were relatively low | Pedestrian characteristics such as age, gender, and distraction (e.g., smartphone use) were not considered in the model | Limited sample size from only five sites prevented some expected variables (PFG/PG length, crosswalk length, elapsed time from PFG onset) from being significant | The model does not consider real interaction between pedestrians and turning vehicles
notes: The study was supported by the Memorandum of Understanding between Qatar Transportation and Traffic Safety Center at Qatar University and the International Center of Urban Safety Engineering at the University of Tokyo. Data was collected using the TrafficAnalyzer video image processing system.

title: Modeling pedestrian crossing speed profiles considering speed change behavior for the safety assessment of signalized intersections
research_problem: Pedestrian safety at signalized intersections is threatened by unexpected sudden speed changes (acceleration/deceleration) that pedestrians exhibit while crossing. These behavioral changes cannot be anticipated by conflicting vehicles, potentially leading to hazardous situations and pedestrian-vehicle conflicts. The study aims to quantitatively model these sudden speed changes to improve safety assessment at signalized crosswalks.
method_summary: The study develops a pedestrian speed profile model using stepwise functions. A discrete choice model (multinomial logit) determines the probability of acceleration, deceleration, or constant speed at each time interval based on factors including necessary speed to complete crossing (Vnec - Vcur), conflict area presence, and signal timing. Separate regression models are used for acceleration (linear regression) and deceleration (normal distribution). Monte Carlo simulation generates complete speed profiles. Data from video surveys at 5 crosswalks in Nagoya, Japan was used.
method_family: Discrete choice model with regression analysis
tasks: Pedestrian speed profile modeling, crossing time estimation, vehicle-pedestrian conflict simulation
datasets: Video survey data from 5 crosswalks at 3 intersections in Nagoya City, Japan (Kanayama, Ueda, Fushimi)
metrics: Modified rho-squared (0.431), Modified R² (acceleration: 0.765, deceleration: 0.725), vehicle yield rate, Post Encroachment Time (PET)
claim_text: Different models are needed for periods before and after PFG onset due to significantly different speed change behaviors
limitations: The accuracy of individual speed-change event detection needs further improvement (23.6% error for non-acceleration, 43.8% for acceleration events) | Pedestrian-pedestrian interaction is not considered because pedestrian traffic volumes at study sites were relatively low | Pedestrian characteristics such as age, gender, and distraction (e.g., smartphone use) were not considered in the model | Limited sample size from only five sites prevented some expected variables (PFG/PG length, crosswalk length, elapsed time from PFG onset) from being significant | The model does not consider real interaction between pedestrians and turning vehicles
notes: The study was supported by the Memorandum of Understanding between Qatar Transportation and Traffic Safety Center at Qatar University and the International Center of Urban Safety Engineering at the University of Tokyo. Data was collected using the TrafficAnalyzer video image processing system.
